# Plugin Repository - AI Agent Instructions

## Overview

This repository contains a scafctl plugin project generated by the plugin template.
It may be either a provider plugin or an auth-handler plugin.

## Key Patterns

- **Plugin SDK**: Uses `github.com/oakwood-commons/scafctl-plugin-sdk`
- **Entry point**: `cmd/<plugin-name>/main.go` is the thin startup layer
- **Implementation**: Core logic belongs in `internal/...`

## MCP Preference

If this workspace has the scafctl MCP server configured in VS Code, prefer MCP
tools over shell commands for scafctl-specific tasks.

- Use MCP for provider and capability discovery: `list_providers`, `get_provider_schema`, `get_provider_output_shape`
- Use MCP for expression and template checks: `validate_expression`, `evaluate_cel`, `evaluate_go_template`
- Use MCP for solution-side debugging when testing this plugin against a scafctl solution: `inspect_solution`, `preview_resolvers`, `preview_action`, `dry_run_solution`
- Fall back to CLI commands when you need the exact user-facing command line, local build output, or behavior outside MCP coverage

## Provider Semantics

When this repository is a provider plugin, keep the provider contract coherent across all methods.

- `GetProviderDescriptor` defines the public contract: name, description, capabilities, schema, and output schemas.
- `OutputSchemas` must include an entry for every declared capability. Missing output schemas cause host registration failure (reported as `provider not found`).
- SDK v0.11.0 enforces required output fields per capability: `action`/`state`/`kubeconfig` need `success` (boolean); `validation` needs `valid` (boolean) and `errors` (array); `authentication` needs `authenticated` (boolean) and `token` (string); `from`/`transform` have none.
- `ExecuteProvider` implements that contract and should reject unknown provider names consistently.
- `DescribeWhatIf` should describe the same action without side effects.
- `ConfigureProvider` should store host configuration, not perform heavy work.
- `ExecuteProviderStream` and `ExtractDependencies` should only be customized when the provider really needs them.

### Provider Naming And Binary Resolution

- The provider name is the RPC contract returned by `GetProviders` and described by `GetProviderDescriptor`, not the raw binary filename.
- Keep the published plugin or catalog name aligned with the provider name users reference in solutions.
- The executable only needs to be a runnable plugin binary. On Windows that means the file must end in `.exe`.
- Prefer binary names like `scafctl-plugin-<provider>` or `scafctl-plugin-<provider>.exe`, but treat that as a convention rather than a loader requirement.
- Do not assume renaming the binary alone will make `provider:<name>` resolve. Provider identity comes from the published artifact name and the plugin RPC responses.

Use `.github/instructions/scafctl-provider.instructions.md` for provider-specific implementation guidance.
Use `.github/prompts/provider-implementation.prompt.md` when implementing provider changes.
Use `.github/prompts/provider-review.prompt.md` when reviewing provider changes.

## Auth-Handler Semantics

When this repository is an auth-handler plugin, keep the auth lifecycle coherent across all methods.

- `GetAuthHandlers` defines the public handler contract: flows, capabilities, and display metadata.
- `Login` establishes authenticated state and should return coherent claims and expiry data.
- `GetStatus` reports auth state without causing login.
- `GetToken` should return a valid token or a clear auth error.
- `ConfigureAuthHandler` should store host configuration rather than doing heavy work.

Use `.github/instructions/scafctl-auth-handler.instructions.md` for auth-handler-specific guidance.
Use `.github/prompts/auth-handler-implementation.prompt.md` when implementing auth-handler changes.
Use `.github/prompts/auth-handler-review.prompt.md` when reviewing auth-handler changes.

## Conventions

- **Commits**: Use [conventional commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/)
- **Signing**: All commits must be GPG/SSH signed (`-S`) and include DCO sign-off (`-s`)
- **Errors**: Return errors with `fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err)`, do not panic for recoverable failures

## Build & Test Commands

~~~bash
task build         # Build the plugin binary
task test          # Run tests
task lint          # Run linter
task lint:fix      # Run linter with auto-fix
task release:local # Package and install locally for end-to-end testing
task ci            # Full CI pipeline (lint + test + build)
~~~

## Local Testing Workflow

The most reliable way to verify a provider works end-to-end is to install it
locally and run a sample solution through the host:

~~~bash
task release:local VERSION=0.1.0
scafctl run solution -f ./examples/solution.yaml
~~~

Direct `--plugin-dir` testing may not exercise the same registration path
as catalog-installed plugins.

## Release and Catalog Publishing

A tagged release must publish both the provider artifact and refresh the
catalog index. Publishing the artifact alone does not make the provider
discoverable.

scafctl reads container registry credentials from `~/.docker/config.json`, so
the release workflow authenticates by writing that file directly:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.docker
AUTH="$(printf '%s' "${GITHUB_ACTOR}:${CATALOG_PUSH_TOKEN}" | base64 -w0)"
printf '{"auths":{"ghcr.io":{"auth":"%s"}}}' "${AUTH}" > ~/.docker/config.json
```

- Use `CATALOG_PUSH_TOKEN` (a PAT with `repo`, `read:packages`, `write:packages`),
  not the repo-scoped `GITHUB_TOKEN`: the index is an org-level package and
  `GITHUB_TOKEN` cannot write it (403).
- Do **not** use `scafctl auth login github --flow pat --write-registry-auth`.
  As of scafctl 0.27.0 that PAT-flow registry bridge fails with
  `tokenprovider: source not found: github` even though login succeeds. The
  direct `~/.docker/config.json` write above is the reliable path.

## Repository Governance

The repository follows scafctl conventions for branch protection and merges.

- **Squash-only merges**: merge commits and rebase merges are disabled. The
  GitHub UI reports "the selected merge method (merge) is not allowed" when a
  merge commit is selected -- that is expected. Use *Squash and merge*. Do not
  suggest enabling merge commits to work around it.
- **Rulesets**: `main` requires a PR review, passing `lint`/`test` checks,
  signed commits, and linear history; version tags (`v*`) are protected from
  deletion and force pushes. Organization admins keep a bypass for force-merges.
- **Applying governance**: repos scaffolded locally (rather than via
  `create_repo=true`) start without these settings. Run `task repo:configure`
  (which runs `scripts/configure-repo.sh`) to apply them.
- **Releases are tag-driven**: `task release:tag VERSION=x.y.z` pushes a signed
  `v*` tag that triggers the release workflow to publish the artifact and
  refresh the catalog index. Do not publish by pushing to `main`.

See [GETTING_STARTED.md](../GETTING_STARTED.md) for the end-to-end setup and
release flow.

## Critical Rules

- **No hardcoded paths**: Use SDK interfaces for host interactions
- **Test coverage**: Every new file should have tests. Target 70%+ patch coverage
- **Git safety**: Never run git commit or push unless explicitly asked
